speaker: interviewer
text: --married Amato; the interviewer, Rebecca Ziman, born [NON-ENGLISH]; tape three. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Are you OK, Madam Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're going to be leaving Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: --very soon, so if there is anything else that you remember that you would like to say about Auschwitz, you know, about the blocks, what they look like, or maybe the bathrooms--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --did it-- was it impressive--

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the morning, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz-- in the morning, they used to call you at 4 o'clock in the morning. And it was so cold. So-- so we used to go to the toilets. You know, it was a big room, very large room, long one, with holes for the derrieres, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we used to go there to keep ourselves warm a little bit, so they-- the-- this is a block over there. How do I say [NON-ENGLISH]? The-- the-- the ones, they were on charge. What-- how I told you? Yes, the lady--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ladies.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the-- yeah, I mean, the prisoners who were in charge of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: The blokova.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the blokovas, yeah. They used to come with a stick to put inside the dirt, to-- to make you go out for the appell, you know? Because we used to be outside for three, four hours standing there and shiver, you see-- no blankets, nothing. And you didn't sleep well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't have, you know, nothing on, only that very thin dress. And it was that one. If you washed it in the night, you go to bed with no-- nothing-- to get dry and put it on, you see? And the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shoes-- no shoes, you see? Look. No shoes at all. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were bare feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bare-- bare feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the snow. Sometimes we used to find, you know, little-- thing like Holland people they left. I don't know. We used to put, but we were not-- no, we had no shoes. We had no shoes. Yeah. This is it, what it was in Auschwitz. And crying--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were explaining to me about disinfection room and the bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it to me what they would look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They terrible, you know. They used to make you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --there to go away, not to stand out-- inside there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did the bathroom look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was a-- a hole--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the only hole for the toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for the shower, for the disinfection?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- no showers-- nothing, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no showers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No showers. There were only pumps, you know. What do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Taps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taps--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --only the taps. And then you used to wash yourself. People, they didn't-- like I told you. They didn't wash very much. But myself, I used to wash with this, with water all the time. Yes, you put it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you soap?

speaker: interviewee
text: No soap. [LAUGHS] They didn't give soap-- nothing. Only when we were in the factory--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they give us, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you didn't know how long you were going to stay in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: --no. We didn't know nothing. We were waiting to die only. Because every time everybody was dying all the time, you know? They say you can't come out from here-- only if the God is going to help you to come out. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they used to take all the dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the crematorium. And people dying, they used to carry. And many time they used to put in a hole the-- one hole they used put all the people and bury them like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who used to make the holes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people, the Jewish people share.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't carry any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: --dead people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How many-- how many prisoners-- women-- were there with you in your block? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the block? Oh, plenty. I don't know. I don't remember. There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it 50? Was it 200? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe about 200. Yeah, there were so many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so it was a big block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you used to sleep how many in one bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six, like sardines, you know? But we used to choose the people, like myself, my sister, Djoya. Because the other one died. My cousin, she was there, and another two friends. They live in America now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you used to sleep--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --close together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, together, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. So now, can you just tell me, how it happened when they told you that you were leaving Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't told us. They told us selection again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we were passing through, we are still in Auschwitz. And then we saw the trains there. So before to go to the trains, they say you must take off your clothes. And then they put us in a-- in a room. We don't know where they were, all these showers, you know? And we didn't know if it was gas or water to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we saw it was coming water, we start to be happy and crying, you know, because it was water-- no gas. We knew already they were killing them with gas, you see? They used to give a piece of bread so you go in-- just now have to wash yourself. And it was the gas. They used to die. They shout and then crying. But when we went there, we saw. We kiss each other, you know, even not sister or friends-- we ourselves, save. And when we finish, they-- we put that dress-- no towels, nothing to dry, nothing. So they put us in the train, you know? And when-- we were so happy. It was midnight-- midnight in the night. Was the train far away from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No it was the same trucks-- the trucks. But at least they had some blankets there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a truck or a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Truck-- truck-- no train, trucks for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trucks, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, where they put the animals. They put us there. And we were going, you know, like sometimes you peep, you see in the pictures, they go in. Then you sit in the floor there. We sit on this-- and shivering. The cold was coming up to here-- burn in that so much, you know, from the cold, you know, and the legs. And then we-- we went about two, three days to arrive to Wilischthal. I don't know how many days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed about how many months, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: All together we finished nearly one year we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but in-- in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was about four-- five to six months. I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they put you in the trucks. And did you know where did-- you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So after--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. About three or four days we arrived in Wilischthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wilischthal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wilischthal, yeah. And we saw that there were beds, you know, with mattresses. But the mattresses, they were [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: Straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Straw. You see? At least we had a little one each. So when we arrived there, we were happy. And they said there is no crematorium there, because there were other people also working there. And we were happy. We had a shower there. And then they give us another dress, you know, to change.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Germany, Wilischthal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start-- they say you must work in the morning, 6 o'clock till 6 o'clock in the night--

speaker: interviewer
text: What work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: --for one week. So I-- I used to-- I used to-- I was in the machine putting there the nails and close it. Like say you're making a-- a ammunition, you know? So you have to put a plug, and they used to make the-- I used to make with the machine, you see? My sister, she used to control them-- if it goes in the thing, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were working-- working in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ammunition factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for one week, daytime. And then, nighttime, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many months were you working there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- still would-- our liberations, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, you stayed there until liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we stayed there, you see? But that-- in that where we went, in that factory, there were many Italians. They're prisoner also. They used to work. So they-- my friend, [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know her. The sister, they were working with the Italian people, you see, inside. And we used to know all the news. They come in. And the English got near. The Germans, they leaving, you know, all these ways to have--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to tell me about your friend, Levy. About--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Rochelle. So in the nighttime, we had-- everybody had the-- the bed, you know? So one day, she-- she pinch a piece of rapa. Rapa is a [NON-ENGLISH]. How-- what do you call? The one you put in the soup--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this white ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, OK, a turnip.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- turnip. She pinched one, and she put it under the bed, you see? But when we used to go to work, they used to look if you've got knives or-- or salt. Because the Italians, they-- they used to be-- they could walk, you see? They used to go. They were in prison, but they were not like the Jewish people, you see? So they used to give to the girls salt or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had that thing. They said, who is this rapa? From who is that thing? So nobody answer. And they see who is sleeping here. It was Leah. Oh, she's going to catch her to put it in the soup, you know, the dishes for soup. They were big and boiling. It was like barrels, you know? And they want to put her. And then she was crying. They wanted to put her in the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the soup. So she was running, running, running. And the sister, she was crying. And she said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH], don't cry. In Spanish she said, [SPEAKING SPANISH]. You know? And then we-- and then we left here ourselves what we used to hear for-- from her. And then, after they didn't catch her, she says she's sick. She went to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the hospital, what she used to do, they used to put in them the [? thermometer. ?] And because she was all right, she was not sick, so-- so-- so she used to take the thermometer and she used to do with her hand to make herself sick, not to come out from there-- because in case they kill her. So like this, she save herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after, we were together. We used to sing. And we met another Italian Jewish girl from Livorno. She was an opera girl, you know. She-- she used to all these songs. And then we used to cry and sing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one night, the [NON-ENGLISH], the blockova, you know, the German came up. She was tall and blond-- said she is singing. She gave her on in the mouth. She broke three teeth from Frieda. It was her name. And she was crying. Beautiful girl-- she was about 21, 22. And she knew all the operas, you know-- opera singer. And she lost all the teeth there, because the [NON-ENGLISH] came and beat her, you see? And after, we used to go sing daytime, you know, and crying. What work were you doing then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I was work-- I was working in the factory. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I knew that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The factory nighttime and daytime. For one week daytime--

speaker: interviewer
text: And one week night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one week nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing now. We used to go-- come to the-- all the time, since they took us to go away from that factory, we used to work every time, everyday. In nighttime we use to sit, but it was not bad like Auschwitz, because I told you we had a-- everybody had their little blankets, you know? And we started to-- a-- a nice Jewish girl-- Polish, with her mother-- they were like sister and daughter. They look like sister-- two sisters. You know, the German, she was in love with her-- German woman. They was making love with her-- the German with the Jewish girl in the block. And we used to see it. They were kissing. She used to bring their food to her-- you know, her food. She used to put it in the little bags they used to have-- and these too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one night when I was working-- we used to work with the-- like I told you, with the Italian, with the Russian. The Russian prisoner near, one day, he gave me his lunch, you know, to me. So I keep it. I put it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had nothing here, you see? Or there was just-- and I said to my sister, come. Let us go to the toilet to warm and eat the thing there. We ate. At least we were a little bit-- and another German-- he was Austrian-- Austrian. He came and he say, ooh, that's good, good, the machine. And he open-- he opened his bag where he had the lunch. He was in-- on duty. He gave me two little apples, you know, from his food, because the-- the Austrian people, they were very nice, you know? You know? So you alternated one week in the day--

speaker: interviewee
text: One--

speaker: interviewer
text: --one week in the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you worked right through until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right through.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the end of the war. OK. What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of the war, these-- when they-- these Germans, they say you must come--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not getting there yet. In this camp that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you go to any other camps at all? Did you go out of the camp to work in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time we were there. We were about 14 of us, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was lucky. We were lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- Do you know-- do you know where this ammunition was going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what they were doing with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- no. I-- we knew we were working for the factory. But we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a German factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: German factory, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And do you know the German, they used to cut the hair to here? And they use to have the blue jackets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because we never really thought to look. I was with the-- you know, Esther [NON-ENGLISH]-- Esther [NON-ENGLISH]. We worked together--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --till there were-- liberation. And Mercardo Chadev-- I was together, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you try and change your identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't want to change your identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still proud to be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you were suffering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I suffered too much, and I cried too much. And where I'm still crying, especially when I came out from the concentration. They used to see me at night to tell the stories. I used to cry all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even once I was in Rome, in the bicycle, and they see me. I was crying. They say, why you crying? They told me in Italian. I said, because I was there. Because they were showing their arm, you know? I said, I was there. I can't believe it-- such a beautiful girl like you. I said, yes, I was there, yeah. Can you just tell me, during the time that you were in the second camp, which was called--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that-- what do you call? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot the name. No, you were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Auschwitz you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to 'Stelevic'. What do you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. You-- you first went to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? From Auschwitz, where did they take you with the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- what do you-- 'Stelevi'-- in the factory. They took us in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but the name of the-- the-- the camp. Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp, yeah, in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what is the name I told you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but you don't remember it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was 'Stelevich'. What do you call? I don't remember. Well, I've got it written down here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My memory, you know? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Madam Lucia, sorry about that. I'm going to read on the document the names of the places that you've been to, because it seems as if you can't remember how to pronounce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said to me that the reason why you know that you went there is because one of your friends that went with you, she remembered the name of the factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went from Auschwitz, Poland on the 16th of August, 1994, you were there plus/minus five to six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you went to Wilischthal--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Germany. And you were there until April, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were sent to a factory in Germany to a place known as Flossenburg Kommander Wilischthal. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the reason why you can't say it is because you can't pronounce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you made an application for your pension--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friends told you that you were going there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was this name. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: From there, can you just-- have you got some more that you would like to talk about from when you were there at the factory? Is there some other stories that you remember maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, making notes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we had stopped at the time we actually said the name of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: What--

speaker: interviewer
text: --factory--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything that you've seen during the time that you were working in the factory? Is there something that you remember that you saw? Where-- you know, what were they doing with the ammunitions? Where was it going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: --what they doing. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't try to find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to work-- yeah-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just went along and worked--

speaker: interviewee
text: And work only time.

speaker: interviewer
text: --shifts--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, shifts.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the morning--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the night, you know? For one week in the morning, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --one week in the nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until-- until you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: At midnight they used to give us a little slice of bread, you know? And-- and the-- the girls, the German they-- to keep up-- wake up, they used to put matches here to the eyes. Because they were tired also-- the Germans ladies. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --can you tell me what happened next when you were still in Wilischthal, when you were working in the factory--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember they used to give, every Sunday, you know, they used to give us some potatoes with cottage cheese-- three potatoes-- with the skin and the bread, and the cottage cheese. And we used to eat it, you know-- so happy it's Sunday-- every Sunday. And then after, we used to take off the skin, we used to eat the skin, because we were hungry. You see? And then one day, one German Jewish, she knew that-- she ran away from the factory. And for three days she was not there. And when they were doing the appell, she was not there. They say, if they don't find her, they are going to kill 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden, they were looking, looking, looking. And she came back, because they caught her in this-- where she went. And they save us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took her. They hit her so much in the head, in the body, the back black-- the head, they made it like [NON-ENGLISH], you know, all of these sores in there. And all-- she was nearly dead in the-- in the floor. And then they say, all the Jews, we must hit her, and we must put cold water on top of her in buckets. And-- and we did this shame. And I thought she's going to finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, after the war, when it was finished, and-- and we were there in the train coming home, we saw her alive. I say, you so lucky. And she was about 45 years old that lady. She was an older lady. But she was a German lady, Jewish, you see? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Where did you go from there? From the-- when-- when you were in the camp in Wilischthal, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there the Germans came. They said, we must go to-- all the people from the factory-- to the train, you know, trains, like, again, the wagons, you know? We went inside there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they open trains or closed trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, closing trains, closing-- only time closing, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: We went up there, and there they were going round and round. And there they were throwing bombs. And we were there. What can we do? We had only water-- for nine days, water and sugar. We survived. You had no food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only for water, you see? And all of a sudden, we saw all the signs are written, Prague-- Prague. We were passing all these villages, you know, and round, and round, and round. All of a sudden, the train stopped, and they left us there. And all the Germans, they used to be with the clothes, soldiers' clothes. They change themselves civilian, and they run away. And they left us there. Many people who were sick. And we were there. All of a sudden, we see people coming. They were-- said, don't be afraid. We are Red Cross. We are Red Cross. We-- they took us to Theresienstadt. From there, they took us--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia. They took us there. And then they-- to give a nice bath, you know, put [? fluid ?] here, all over. Because if you sick, you are sick. In any case, they took care of us. They give us a nice clothes, nightie, a coat, and [NON-ENGLISH], all this. And then I said to my sister, I'm going to go around again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- in Theresienstadt, the-- the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but do you know who they were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish. They said they're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said that time they put-- also in there-- they-- they had also crematorium there, ready to-- to kill all these Jewish that were there. But the-- the Russians arrive. And they were fighting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we were there, they-- they took us to Theresienstadt. Still they were fighting. On the 8th of May, it's finished, the war. You know? How did you know that it was finished, the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they says, finished. You know, they were talking, the other people. This is finished-- is finished. And they were shooting. And-- and the German-- the Jewish people, they start taking the Germans like prisoners-- you know, the Jewish people, the ones that were in Theresienstadt. We were sick, you know. We were thin. We had no strength. So they give us nice food, nice soup, for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you wanted to go anywhere, you used to show the arm with the number. You didn't pay no water, no cafe, nothing, you see? They used to give you for nothing, these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after two weeks, we were in Theresienstadt there, two, three weeks, I was walking, and I saw my cousin, Clara. She was in the typhus verboten. You can't go near. She had the typhus. After a few weeks, she was better. She came with us, with that-- where we were, the Jewish people, like Matilda, Israel. All these people, they were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a camp, Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt, it was a very big camp. But they gave us nice food. The Red Cross, they give us parcels.

speaker: interviewer
text: As soon as we start eating, our period came. And we used to make food, fete, you know, dancing and singing, you know, from the joys, you know? We had the-- the thing. [SPEAKING SPANISH]. Shame. She was young. One-- one of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't get her periods anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore. And she was-- she was 16, 17. She was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you-- were you aware that they were putting-- they were giving you things to stop the period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they used to put-- yeah, they put [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me, the people there-- the French, that lady. She said they put things, no, not to come that thing, you know? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it in your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the food they use to put, but we didn't know nothing, you see? Just went there maybe we were malnoutritions. We had no food, that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did some of the people-- their hair fall out or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, I haven't got very much, on-- very fine hair, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And rheumatism terrible, you know, from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You suffering now from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ooh! A lot-- my legs, my foot, and my arms, you know, from buckets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to walk a lot on the-- on the snow with no shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with no shoes-- nothing. And we were-- and we never saw the snow in Rhodos. Now there is snow everywhere. But before, they didn't. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Theresienstadt was mainly like a recovery--

speaker: interviewee
text: Recovery. People--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was Jewish-- the-- all the Jewish people there. You know, it was very nice. They treat us very nice. After--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't work there in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no working-- sitting, eating all day, you know? They gave us chocolate. They gave us garlic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- the Russians, yes. They throw away cigarettes to us, chocolates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you something? Do you remember at the present moment in time, when you actually were told that it was-- there was no more war, how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so happy. And then what we did is-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know, Rochelle, she was married to Flor's brother, Kapuluto. So now-- she said now is the time we are going to go and-- and-- and pinch things. So we pinch all these pillow cases. And it was all plumas, what do you call? All--

speaker: interviewer
text: Feathers.

speaker: interviewee
text: --feathers. We used to-- and then from that material, we used to make shirts for us, you know, to change ourselves. And it was very nice, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we saw all the Germans running like the Jewish people. And we were-- many people, they throw stones to the German. But I didn't. I didn't throw them, you know? Do you have a hatred toward the Germans now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because you know, there it was the war, you know? Many Germans are nice-- the one-- the-- the ones that are not SS, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you got friends that are Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Here there is two, three people here they live. They are German. They are very nice, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you friendly with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't say nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --begrudge?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you friendly with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And does it matter to you if you buy an item that's German labeled?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --buy this, because here they don't sell some of-- Yes, but it doesn't matter to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that the Germans, you know, destroy ours family, and you know, all these people kill. But you can't do nothing when the war, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many from your sisters-- the four of you that went-- was it four sisters and the brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you survived the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The three. We came back, three-- Djoya, myself, and Mauricio, who died also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was there, he died young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he died where? In-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Rome. He died in Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After. Yeah, he got married, then he got two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can tell me that just now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Theresienstadt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: About-- you wrote in the document that you stayed until the 8th of May, 1945. That's was the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated there at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed three two, three weeks, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: After?

speaker: interviewee
text: After. And then they start-- they start coming to take us home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Say, where do you want to go? To Italy. But Italy--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel then? Did-- where did you-- where--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were happy to go home, to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go which home?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Rome, you see, because I had my sister there. You see.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel that you had no, you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: No people to go nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived in Milan for 10 days, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go to Milan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like that I said, from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. From--

speaker: interviewee
text: We took the trains. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Theresienstadt you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Theresienstadt--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we left. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we were going-- ah, you left the telephone. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. From the Theresienstadt, you took a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- they put them on the train, the people. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who put you in a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPANISH]? Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just stop for a minute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop, eh? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madam Lucia, you said that you were looked after still in the camp of Theresienstadt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by the Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were put in hospitals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Everything they give us. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- put powder to take the fleas.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fleas, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, the-- now, from the-- whose decision was it for you to go to Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the people they say, you are Italian. You must go over there in Italy. So they put us in the train. Instead--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they? Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people, the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we went-- we were in Vienna. The-- the-- the Russians came, and we changed the-- the trains. We're-- we take about 12 with us. Other people, we lost them. So they went to Paris and things. We went to Milan after the liberation, you see? And we arrived in Milan. And the doctors look at after you, see if you've got cough, if you've got TB, all the things they look out-- the Italians. Who-- who received you in Milan?

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctors there, when we arrived there, the-- the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came there, and the-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also-- also Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Red Cross, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Rome-- in-- in Milan?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Milan we stay about 10 days. And I say I want to look to Rome, because I've got a sister there. And I didn't know there there isn't things. And after--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was the sister that got married before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Si.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived with the four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was in Rome, and we went there to live with there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1945 till 1947.
